policy_statement: By 2024, apply approximately 500 wet tons of biosolids per year as a soil amendment to sequester carbon on identified sites such as mine reclamation projects in Northern California (HE.7-1).
mechanism_description: The policy plans to apply biosolids as a soil amendment on identified sites (e.g., mine reclamation projects) to increase carbon stored in soils.
primary_causal_pathway: Organic amendment application → ↑ soil organic matter formation/stabilization → ↑ soil carbon stocks (carbon sink) → ↓ net atmospheric CO₂
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in activity/system: Land managers apply treated wastewater biosolids to soils (e.g., reclaimed mine lands, degraded sites) as an organic soil amendment, often coupled with site preparation and vegetation establishment.  
(2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Added organic inputs are incorporated into soil and partially stabilized via aggregation and mineral association; improved soil conditions can increase plant growth and belowground carbon inputs, further building soil organic carbon.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net increase in terrestrial carbon sequestration (higher soil carbon stocks) reduces net GHG concentrations, subject to managing potential countervailing emissions (e.g., N₂O from added nitrogen, CH₄ if applied under anaerobic conditions).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is increasing net carbon stored in soils by adding and stabilizing organic carbon from biosolids, thereby removing CO₂ from the atmosphere over time.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism’s core pathway is enhancement of a biological/soil carbon sink through organic amendment application leading to increased soil carbon storage.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although biosolids reuse can have Resource Efficiency elements (diverting organic residuals from disposal) and can affect Mitigation via avoided landfill emissions, the stated and mechanism-defining effect here is *soil carbon sequestration* (sink enhancement), which is most directly Nature-Based Solutions.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a policy’s primary intent/effect were disposal diversion (avoided landfill CH₄) or fertilizer displacement (avoided industrial N production), the primary category could shift; however, those are not intrinsic to this canonical mechanism definition focused on soil carbon sequestration.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that applies biosolids specifically to increase soil carbon stocks relies on ecosystem/soil processes to store additional carbon, fitting Nature-Based Solutions across contexts (reclamation, rangeland, forestry, degraded soils).  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Co-benefits/risks (e.g., avoided disposal emissions, fertilizer substitution, N₂O tradeoffs, water retention/adaptation benefits) require policy-specific evidence and are deferred to policy-level assessment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential countervailing emissions (notably N₂O from nitrogen additions, CH₄ under waterlogged conditions) can reduce net climate benefit; if these dominate in a given implementation, the net effect may be smaller. Some programs may frame biosolids land application primarily as waste diversion or nutrient recycling; those should be classified by their dominant quantified pathway, but this canonical mechanism is defined by soil carbon sequestration.
key_indicators: “By 2024”; “apply approximately 500 wet tons of biosolids per year”; “as a soil amendment”; “to sequester carbon”; “newly identified sites such as mine reclamation projects in Northern California”
co_benefits: Soil rehabilitation/improved soil fertility and structure; mine land reclamation and erosion control; potential waste diversion/beneficial reuse of biosolids
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be implemented via procurement/contracts or a program requirement, but the explicit quantified goal and deadline make “target-setting” the best fit based on the text alone.
